source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: Talent acquisition and retention is the second great arms race in enterprise, parallel to customer acquisition. With millions of unfilled software roles, companies that build better talent pipelines gain a durable structural advantage.
original_quote: The second massive arms race that's underway besides customer acquisition is talent acquisition. We see this particularly in categories like software development where there's millions of unfilled jobs in the US alone and they can't find them. The battle for talent is global now.
implication: HR tech and talent intelligence platforms have the same structural importance as CRM and sales tools. Investors and founders should treat talent infrastructure as a core competitive moat.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: Vertical SaaS platforms are deceptively large opportunities because they can capture 5-10% of total customer revenue by offering soup-to-nuts functionality — versus the basis-point take rates of horizontal enterprise software like Salesforce.
original_quote: A vertical SaaS provider can take probably 5 to 10% of total revenue and that's a win for the customer because they're able to grow their business dramatically versus what they were with way fewer people. Whereas Salesforce's percentage of revenue from a Fortune 500 customer is probably not even one basis point.
implication: TAM calculations for vertical SaaS must account for revenue penetration rates, not just market headcount. A seemingly small vertical can generate outsized revenue when the platform becomes the operating system of that industry.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: Modern enterprises no longer want one monolithic software system. Instead they want best-of-breed solutions that integrate with existing systems of record — making API-first architecture a non-negotiable requirement for new enterprise software.
original_quote: Enterprises in the future are now past wanting one monolithic piece of software like SAP to run everything. They're now matching best-of-breed providers that bring a certain level of functionality to the table but can still now integrate with the general ledger and the CRM system and the other core systems of record.
implication: API-first design is no longer a technical preference — it is the price of admission to the modern enterprise stack. Software that cannot integrate will be bypassed regardless of its standalone quality.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: Enterprise software buyers now demand fast time-to-value — measurable impact in 3-6 months — not 18-month implementation cycles. Startups that deliver rapid demonstrable value have a structural advantage over legacy enterprise vendors.
original_quote: They want to be able to do it with a specific short-term time to value in mind where it's not this like 18 months and hope that it works kind of type implementation. It's got to be clear value in three months, six months at the most.
implication: Speed-to-value is now a core product design constraint for enterprise software, not just a sales consideration. Products that require long implementation cycles will lose to faster, more modular alternatives.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: Contracts are the sixth and largely untapped system of record — containing the underlying data for virtually every revenue, expense, asset, and liability in a company — yet most organizations leave this data locked in PDFs or file cabinets.
original_quote: Almost every revenue and expense item that flows through your P&L, or every asset and liability on your balance sheet, is sitting in your contracts. That underlying data is the underlying asset value of all those contracts — and it's not accessible, it's not searchable, it's not analyzable to improve your performance.
implication: Contract intelligence platforms like Icertis represent a massive untapped category. The company that becomes the system of record for contracts owns a dataset that touches every financial dimension of an enterprise.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: You no longer need to be the system of record to have an unfair data advantage. API access means analytics and intelligence companies can build powerful products on top of existing systems of record without displacing them.
original_quote: The ability to get the data through these APIs now exists — you don't even have to necessarily be the system of record player anymore to have an unfair advantage on getting that data.
implication: The most interesting enterprise software opportunities may be analytical and workflow layers built on top of existing systems, not replacements for them. Competitive moats can be built on data access, not data ownership.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: The ideal venture investment pattern is participating in every funding round from seed through pre-IPO. This continuous commitment requires both conviction and patience, and reflects a genuine 10-year partnership mindset.
original_quote: The perfect scenario for us is we invest in every single round of the company whether we're leading it or not — we want to play a significant role. We're looking 10 years out thinking is this the category-leading company that's building a business that's got the legs to be a public business.
implication: Venture firms that can follow their conviction through every round build deeper relationships and capture more of the value creation curve. For founders, this signals a partner with genuine long-term alignment.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: Goldman Sachs's senior partners in its private-partnership era modeled the importance of relationship capital — playing a long game, maintaining emotional intelligence across all levels of an organization, and investing in people before transactions.
original_quote: The senior partners were incredibly balanced — they had perspective and they could talk to people all the way down to guys like me as an intern. It made me realize how important those relationships are to the basis of all business relationships as well as of course your personal relationships.
implication: Relationship capital compounds over decades in ways that transactional behavior never can. The long game in business — as in investing — is built on consistent, high-EQ relationship investment.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: Portfolio-wide pattern recognition is a powerful competitive advantage for established venture funds. Seeing what works and what doesn't across dozens of companies gives investors an empirical basis for sector mapping that first-time investors cannot replicate.
original_quote: We have a large portfolio so we see across our portfolio what's working in certain companies and what are some that are for whatever reason have pointed into the wind a bit. We're triangulating all those different elements into where's the current going.
implication: Scale in venture creates compounding informational advantages. Founders choosing investors should consider whether the fund's portfolio insights and pattern recognition are relevant to their specific category.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: The five traditional enterprise systems of record — CRM, HCM, ERP, service management, and general ledger — are largely claimed. The next wave of value creation comes from analytics, workflow, and intelligence layers built around these anchors.
original_quote: There's CRM, there's HR — Workday — so there's Salesforce, there's Workday, there's Zendesk and ServiceNow, and then there's obviously the general ledger players and then there's an ERP system. Those are kind of generally thought of as the five systems of record.
implication: Enterprise software founders should map their product against the existing systems-of-record landscape before positioning. The opportunity is increasingly in the intelligence and workflow layer, not in displacing entrenched systems.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: The early investor relationship is underappreciated as a critical strategic decision. As companies stay private longer, investors joining boards at seed stage may be with the founding team for a decade — making human fit as important as capital.
original_quote: It puts the onus on the investor and the entrepreneur to spend more time together understanding — is that relationship really going to be a reciprocally good one on a human level besides just the terms of the deal. I think that's a really underappreciated part of the business.
implication: Due diligence should flow both ways. Founders evaluating term sheets should invest deeply in understanding the investor's operating style, availability, and long-term patience before signing.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: Cross-disciplinary insight between consumer and enterprise investing creates unexpected advantages. Trends visible in consumer behavior often presage enterprise software needs, and vice versa.
original_quote: Even surprisingly, sometimes listening to our consumer guys and them going through their things actually gives us insight into some of the enterprise models that we're thinking about. That cross-functional disciplinary action of our firm is pretty helpful.
implication: Firms and individuals who maintain visibility across both consumer and enterprise domains can identify enterprise opportunities earlier by reading consumer trend signals. Siloed thinking leaves value on the table.
source_ref: #29 Greycroft: Investing Along the Frontier of Cloud Platforms and Enterprise Software | Mark Ter...

content: Behance's creative profile concept — all of a creator's public work collected in one place — became the foundation for Adobe Creative Cloud's unified profile system. Integrating community into the tools people already use makes sharing work and getting feedback a natural part of the creative process rather than an afterthought.
original_quote: The creative profile which was certainly a theme of today's demos actually got its Origins with the behance portfolio and this notion of all of your public work being collected in one place... integrating Community into the tools that you use make sharing your work and getting feedback and attribution a more natural part of the creative process.
implication: When community and feedback are embedded directly into creative workflows rather than siloed on a separate platform, creators are more likely to share earlier and iterate faster.

content: When work is shared earlier in the creative process — before it's finished — the community can help improve it, making the final output better. This shifts the value of a creative platform from a showcase of completed work to an active improvement engine.
original_quote: They're actually getting uploaded earlier in the process which means the creative Community is helping everyone that's posting uh improve the work before it's finished so really starting to Leverage The Creative community in the finished work itself.
implication: Platforms that encourage in-progress sharing rather than only finished portfolios unlock a collaborative quality improvement loop that benefits both individual creators and the broader community.

content: A talent marketplace emerged organically on Behance without being explicitly designed — creators posted portfolios while job-seeking, and recruiters joined to find talent. Recognizing and then formalizing this emergent behavior into a dedicated Talent Search product is an example of platform strategy following user behavior.
original_quote: Some members we're seeing more and more members now joining and posting portfolios as they're looking for a job and we're seeing more and more people creative directors and recruiters joining behance to find and hire creative Talent so in effect a talent Market was starting to take shape.
implication: The best platform features are often discovered by watching how users already behave rather than invented top-down — platform builders should instrument their communities to detect emergent use cases before competitors do.

content: Talent search based on portfolios rather than resumes can foster greater meritocracy in the creative world. When discovery is based on the actual quality and style of work rather than credentials or connections, skill becomes the primary filter.
original_quote: Talent search capitalizes in the portfolio rather than the traditional resume so by organizing and indexing portfolios in real time we can help creatives get discovered in new ways and and possibly Foster more meritocracy in the Creative World.
implication: Shifting hiring infrastructure away from resumes toward portfolio-based discovery could systematically reduce credentialism in creative fields and surface talent that traditional gatekeepers would miss.

content: The 'creative graph' — a data structure capturing every public project, community curation signals, and relationships between top creatives across fields — enables recommendation algorithms that improve the more the network is used. This is a proprietary data asset that becomes more valuable with scale.
original_quote: Talent search lets us tap what we like to call the creative graph for recommendations with data about every public project published, community curation and the relationships between top creatives in every field we've developed recommendation algorithms to help match creatives with opportunity and your recommendations only improve as you discover more people.
implication: A creative graph is a defensible competitive moat — the more creators and recruiters use the platform, the more accurate the recommendations become, creating a self-reinforcing network effect that is difficult for competitors to replicate.

content: Visual similarity matching — using image science to find projects and creators stylistically similar to a given piece of work — is more powerful than keyword or category search for creative talent discovery. It surfaces talent that would never self-identify under the right label.
original_quote: This is not just a list short list of people who have designated themselves as children's book illustrators this is really looking at the image science and finding similarity matching.
implication: For creative work, visual and stylistic similarity search unlocks discovery that text-based search fundamentally cannot — platforms that invest in image recognition as a search layer gain a qualitative advantage in matching creative talent to opportunities.

content: Behance's Talent Search extended beyond its own website to include portfolio networks for top art and design schools and thousands of personal portfolio sites, dramatically expanding its reach. Platform value scales when the infrastructure powers a broader ecosystem rather than just a single destination.
original_quote: Talent search provide search capabilities for the full behance platform so much more than just behance's website including dozens of portfolio networks powered by beans for top Art and Design schools like Ry and scad and Pratt as well as thousands of other personal portfolio sites.
implication: By becoming the infrastructure layer for the broader creative portfolio ecosystem rather than just one destination, Behance expanded its network effects and data collection surface — a classic platform strategy move.

content: Adobe's strategic insight was to connect Creative Cloud with Marketing Cloud, creating a pipeline from creative talent discovery to campaign execution. The marketing department — the leading employer of creative professionals, spending $3 billion annually on creative hiring — becomes the fulcrum of this integration.
original_quote: The leading employer for Creative professionals is the marketing department... they spend $3 billion dollar a year hiring creative Talent now many of those marketing departments use Adobe marketing Cloud to run and track their campaigns so the teams are hard at work combining our marketing cloud and our Creative Cloud so it's much easier for Creative Cloud members to find jobs.
implication: Connecting the supply side (Creative Cloud creators) with the demand side (Marketing Cloud customers) through a unified talent layer creates a powerful flywheel — Adobe captures value at both ends of the creative labor market.

content: Sharing a personal passion project publicly — even one as speculative as a concept redesign of a major product — can generate significant professional opportunity. The creative community rewards genuine creative work with visibility, freelance work, and career-changing attention.
original_quote: A lot of things changed when I posted this personal project on behance called Facebook facelift um which was like this concept of a different way to think about Facebook and how it's structured and how it looks and I was kind of like overwhelmed with the feedback I was getting a lot of freelance work just came in as a result of that.
implication: For creatives, public portfolio work often outperforms traditional job-seeking — a single well-executed speculative project can function as a more effective career signal than any resume or application.

content: For creative hiring, the work itself is always more informative than a resume. Recruiters and creative directors consistently prioritize seeing actual work over credentials, making portfolio platforms the natural infrastructure for creative talent markets.
original_quote: You can look at a resume till you blue in the face but ultimately it's the work that is what you're interested in and so behance is the best platform for the work that we're looking for.
implication: Any platform that makes it easier to evaluate actual creative work — rather than proxies like education or job titles — will win in the creative talent market, because it aligns the evaluation method with what hiring decision-makers actually care about.

content: Behance functions simultaneously as a professional tool and as a source of personal inspiration — members use it not just to advance their careers but because they enjoy consuming great creative work. Platforms that serve both professional utility and genuine enjoyment achieve deeper user engagement.
original_quote: I use behance not just professionally but just because I like looking at good work.
implication: When a platform creates intrinsic value beyond its instrumental purpose — when people use it even when they're not job-seeking or hiring — it builds habitual engagement that is far stickier than purely transactional platforms.

content: Behance is a professional community grounded in the sharing of creative work by practitioners — not a general social network. This professional, work-focused character distinguishes it from broader platforms and makes it more useful as a discovery and hiring tool.
original_quote: It's a nice Community because it's really based on on professionals sharing their ideas... behance is always the first place we start.
implication: Niche professional communities with a clear shared identity and purpose often outperform general platforms for specific use cases — depth of relevance beats breadth of reach when the task requires judgment about quality.

content: Connecting mobile apps directly to community — enabling publishing work-in-progress from mobile to a portfolio platform — reduces the friction between creation and sharing to near zero. Lower friction at the point of sharing leads to earlier and more frequent uploads.
original_quote: The creative profile opens up all kinds of possibilities for integrating behance into creative workflows like quickly publishing work in progress from your desktop and mobile apps adding portfolio projects directly from many of the mobile apps.
implication: Product teams should think of the share/publish action as a core feature to optimize — every reduction in friction between creation and community sharing increases the volume and velocity of content that feeds the platform's network effects.

content: Generative AI empowers creative professionals to explore a wider design space more quickly, rather than replacing them. It acts as a force multiplier, allowing creatives to test more possibilities and arrive at better ultimate solutions for clients and brands.
original_quote: generative AI is going to make professionals more powerful. It's going to allow them to Explore More Design space of possibility, play with more possibilities and find the best Ultimate solution for their customer or for their brand.
implication: The competitive advantage of AI for creatives is not automation of output but acceleration of exploration — those who use it to iterate faster will consistently outperform those who don't.

content: Human creative confidence peaks at age five and declines as social comparison and criticism take hold. Generative AI has the potential to reverse this trend by lowering the barrier to visual self-expression for everyone.
original_quote: humans are most confident creatively when they're five years old. And this is when we're drawing and everything we draw our parents will put up on the refrigerator... as you get older you realize that a lot of people around you have skills that you don't have and suddenly you start to recoil and your creative confidence goes down.
implication: AI-driven creative tools could unlock a latent creative population that self-selected out of creative work due to skill gaps — representing a massive expansion of the creative economy.

content: In the age of AI-driven productivity, standing out requires creativity rather than efficiency. The competitive differentiator has shifted from doing things faster to generating better ideas — creativity is the new productivity.
original_quote: we're in a new era where productivity is being achieved through compute — computers and AI in particular are doing a lot of the productivity-driving work for us and we need to be more creative in order to be more successful in our careers.
implication: Individuals and organizations that invest in creative capacity now will be better positioned as AI commoditizes routine knowledge work — creativity becomes the scarce, valuable input.

content: Creativity is shifting from a solo endeavor to a collaborative, stakeholder-driven process. As creativity becomes the primary competitive advantage for companies, more people want involvement — which forces creative professionals to build transparency and collaboration into their workflows.
original_quote: creativity is the competitive advantage of most companies and Brands... that means that everyone wants to be a stakeholder of creativity and that's a little scary for us creative Pros that don't want people over our shoulders.
implication: Tools that enable collaborative creativity — real-time co-editing, structured feedback loops, multiplayer canvases — will become essential infrastructure as creativity moves from individual to organizational sport.

content: Generative AI does not replace taste or originate ideas — it accelerates the exploration of ideas a human already has. The human's role shifts from executor to curator and discerner of possibilities.
original_quote: generative AI doesn't replace taste and it doesn't give you ideas but it lets you explore ideas more quickly.
implication: Taste and judgment become the scarcest and most valuable human inputs in creative workflows augmented by AI — those with superior discernment will leverage AI to dramatically outpace those without it.

content: When adopting transformative new technology, Belsky recommends a four-stage framework beginning with Play, then Protect (incubating ideas), then Pilot (testing on a real project), then Provoke (asking hard questions about what it changes). Novelty precedes utility — play is the legitimate first step.
original_quote: I stay see four things that start with a P — the first is play... novelty precedes utility in new technologies... the second thing is to protect the ability to incubate some ideas... the third is to find an opportunity to Pilot... and then the fourth is about provoking difficult questions.
implication: Organizations that skip directly to deployment without a play-and-pilot phase miss the learning that makes adoption durable — structured experimentation reduces the risk of either over-investing or dismissing transformative tools prematurely.

content: The future of digital experience is hyper-personalized — websites that greet users by name, applications that meet users where they are rather than requiring users to learn the application, and search replaced by proactive suggestions. AI makes this level of personalization technically feasible.
original_quote: I think that websites will greet Us by name, I think that instead of us always having to learn how an application works an application will sort of meet us where we are... I think about a world we don't have to search anymore for something but rather we can just ask or get suggestions.
implication: The design paradigm shifts from users adapting to software to software adapting to users — products that deliver this experience will create dramatically higher retention and loyalty.

content: Personalization powered by AI must be paired with transparency — users need to understand how content is personalized and where their data comes from. Trust is the prerequisite for personalization at scale.
original_quote: we need to know how they're personalized for each of us we can't be skeptical of where the data is coming from there needs to be a lot of transparency around how this content was made and around how it knows who we are.
implication: Companies that build explainable, transparent personalization systems will earn durable user trust; those that personalize opaquely will face backlash and regulatory exposure.

content: AI-generated content that lacks human soul and emotional authenticity fails to move audiences, even when technically proficient. The Casey Neistat experiment — executing a ChatGPT-scripted vlog to the letter — demonstrated that soulless content is recognizably hollow.
original_quote: he said the reason it was so bad is because it lacked soul. And I think that was a really important message that I think a lot of artists and a lot of consumers of content are realizing now.
implication: As AI floods channels with technically competent but emotionally empty content, human-originated work with genuine emotional resonance will command a premium in attention and trust.

content: The uniquely human elements of creativity — emotional background, lived experience, counter-intuitive impulses, idiosyncratic perspective — cannot be replicated by machines. These become the premium differentiator as AI content generation scales.
original_quote: The counter-intuitive nature of humanity, the emotion, The Edge, the things that move us, the mistakes of the eye that a creative has or the emotional background, the experiences that makes them come up with something new — that can't be done and it will never be done by machines.
implication: Creatives should lean into what makes their work distinctly human rather than competing with AI on volume or speed — emotional depth, personal experience, and idiosyncratic point of view are irreproducible moats.

content: As AI enables mass content generation, the market will be flooded with undifferentiated, soulless output. Human creativity with genuine emotional resonance becomes rarer and therefore more valuable — the premium of humanity increases as AI content volume increases.
original_quote: that's sort of the premium of human creativity that I think will become even more important in this age where everyone's sort of flooding The Zone with content generation from computers.
implication: The signal-to-noise problem in content will intensify dramatically, and audiences will develop stronger filters for authenticity — creators and brands that invest in genuine human expression will stand out in an ocean of AI-generated content.

content: 3D design tools are becoming approachable for non-specialists, democratizing a previously math-intensive domain. This lowers barriers in the same way generative AI lowered barriers to image creation — expanding the population of capable 3D creators.
original_quote: 3D used to be this very daunting scary kind of math intensive world that most people felt they couldn't play in... it's very approachable with products like Substance Painter — you don't even need to know how to design and make the materials and textures to use them.
implication: Creative professionals who upskill in 3D now will be positioned to contribute to the next wave of immersive experiences before the tools become fully commoditized.

content: New spatial computing devices like Apple Vision Pro will create demand for 3D creative assets and experiences, making 3D literacy an important skill for the next generation of creative professionals.
original_quote: With the emergence of new devices like The Vision Pro that Apple just released, we're going to want to create three-dimensional assets and experiences and so I encourage all creators to start to use this technology to learn how it works so that when these experiences come online we can all contribute to them.
implication: Creators who develop 3D skills now before the spatial computing market matures will have a significant head start when demand surges — early platform literacy historically translates to outsized opportunity.

content: Content production should not be confused with creativity. Volume of output and mechanical execution of instructions — even from sophisticated AI — does not constitute creative work. Creativity requires human emotional intent behind it.
original_quote: some people will confuse content production with creativity. Just producing or copying-pasting content is not what moves us emotionally to buy something, to read something, to think differently — it doesn't change us. Whereas creativity that has emotion behind it is what makes it effective.
implication: Marketers and creators who mistake AI-generated output volume for creative strategy will see diminishing returns — effectiveness metrics will increasingly separate emotional resonance from content throughput.

content: Adobe's approach to generative AI is creator-centric — building capabilities that amplify what creative professionals can do rather than bypassing them. The reception to Generative Fill in Photoshop confirmed that professionals embrace AI when it genuinely extends their capabilities.
original_quote: our team took a very creator-centric approach in bringing some of these capabilities into Photoshop... I think the reception has certainly exceeded our imagination — what people are doing with it, how they're changing their process to incorporate it.
implication: Products that frame AI as professional empowerment rather than replacement earn faster adoption and deeper loyalty from skilled user bases — the framing of the value proposition matters as much as the capability itself.

content: Playful, skeptic-to-believer journeys with new technology are valuable signals. When initially resistant users begin experimenting and find personal meaning in the technology, it marks genuine utility rather than hype-driven adoption.
original_quote: I saw one social feed from an artist who was at first very skeptical of this and then he started to remove himself from all of his self-portraits — this is the photographer named Noah Kalina — and suddenly he became kind of fascinated with it. It was interesting to see his process of play.
implication: Watching how skeptics convert — not early adopters — reveals the authentic use cases of transformative technology and provides the most credible evidence for broader adoption.

content: Generative AI will enable unprecedented personalization of content at scale — producing variants tailored for different audiences far faster than human teams can. This reshapes both content strategy and the economics of creative production.
original_quote: it will allow us to personalize content for more people more quickly and I see it being a real accelerant to kind of what creative people are capable of.
implication: Organizations that develop workflows for AI-assisted content personalization will reach audiences with far greater relevance and efficiency — redefining what a lean creative team can produce.
